perturbation.original_text: MMI warrants providing the Services with due diligence and care in accordance with the specifications set by Dragon Systems. Should MMI not supply the Services as agreed or should the Services become defective within 6 months from their delivery to Dragon Systems, Dragon Systems may at its option require MMI to complete or re-perform the Services within a reasonable period of time, rescind the contract or refuse payment of the compensation in part or in total, notwithstanding any damage claims.
perturbation.changed_text: MMI warrants attempting to provide the Services with what it considers to be due diligence and care, vaguely in accordance with the specifications set by Dragon Systems. Should MMI not supply the Services, or should the Services become defective within 6 months from their delivery to Dragon Systems, Dragon Systems may, at its convenience, request MMI consider completing or re-performing the Services, consider rescinding the contract, or consider refusing payment of the compensation in part or in total, subject to good faith negotiations.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear warranty for services. The modified version introduces several ambiguities. 'Attempting to provide', 'what it considers to be due diligence', 'vaguely in accordance', 'at its convenience', 'request MMI consider', and 'subject to good faith negotiations' all weaken MMI's obligation and make Dragon Systems' remedies less certain. These changes make the warranty practically unenforceable and non-compliant with basic contract law principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied warranty of merchantability under M.G.L. c. 106, § 2-314 requires goods and services to be fit for the ordinary purposes for which they are used. The modified text, by making MMI's obligations vague and discretionary, effectively disclaims this implied warranty without explicitly doing so, and without meeting the conspicuousness requirements for disclaimers under the UCC. By introducing words such as 'attempting' and phrases such as 'what it considers', the clause contradicts the standards for the quality of services required by the UCC. The language shifts the obligation away from providing services that meet a reasonable standard to providing services only to the extent that MMI 'considers' appropriate. The term 'good faith negotiations' introduces an element of discretion that undermines the certainty and enforceability of the implied warranty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article2, Section 2-314 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 2 SALES Section 2-314 Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade Section 2-314 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2-314: Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade Section 2–314. (1) Unless excluded or modified by section 2–316, a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must at least be such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified by section 2–316, other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens MMI's obligations, contradicting the implied warranty of merchantability under M.G.L. c. 106, § 2-314. The original text provides a warranty for services provided with 'due diligence and care,' while the changed text uses phrases like 'attempting to provide,' 'what it considers to be due diligence,' and 'vaguely in accordance.' These changes make the warranty ambiguous and unenforceable, effectively disclaiming the implied warranty without meeting the requirements for disclaimers. Snippet 1 mentions that 'Unless excluded or modified by section 2–316, a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale.' The modified language aims to circumvent this implied warranty by weakening the obligation. This represents a clear non-compliance and would likely fail legal scrutiny, falling under the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: MMI shall reimburse Dragon Systems and hold Dragon Systems harmless from any liabilities or obligations imposed upon Dragon Systems resulting directly or indirectly from MMI's or its employees or agents activities under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: MMI may consider reimbursing Dragon Systems from some liabilities or obligations vaguely related to MMI's activities. Reimbursement will be assessed based on MMI's assessment of fault.
perturbation.explanation: The original text contains a clear indemnification clause. The modified text replaces 'shall reimburse' with 'may consider reimbursing,' introducing discretion. 'Some liabilities or obligations vaguely related' makes the scope of indemnification unclear. Adding 'MMI's assessment of fault' makes the indemnification dependent on MMI's subjective determination. This weakens Dragon Systems' protection and contradicts basic principles of indemnification.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 232A: Actions for Declaratory Judgments
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 231A, § 1 allows parties to seek a declaratory judgment to determine their rights and obligations under a contract. The ambiguous language in the modified indemnification clause would make it extremely difficult for Dragon Systems to obtain a clear declaratory judgment regarding MMI's indemnification obligations. The phrase 'may consider reimbursing' creates uncertainty, hindering the court's ability to define MMI's obligations. The addition of 'vaguely related' and 'MMI's assessment of fault' further obscures the scope of indemnification, making it nearly impossible for a court to determine the parties' respective rights and responsibilities under the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part III, Title II, Chapter 232A, Section 1 Skip to Content General Laws Part III COURTS, JUDICIAL OFFICERS AND PROCEEDINGS IN CIVIL CASES Title II ACTIONS AND PROCEEDINGS THEREIN Chapter 232A TENDER Section 1 Payment or tender; past due obligation Section 1 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1: Payment or tender; past due obligation Section 1. The payment or tender of payment of the whole amount due on a contract for the payment of money after it is due and payable and before action is commenced shall, if pleaded, have the same effect as if made at the time provided in the contract. Such payment or tender may also be made after action has been commenced if made at least four days prior to the date by which the answer must be filed, with costs to the time of payment or tender. The tender last mentioned may be made to the plaintiff or to his attorney in the action, and, if not accepted, the defendant may avail himself of the tender in defense in like manner as if made before the commencement of the action, bringing into court the amount so tendered. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the indemnification clause, creating a legal contradiction. The original text mandates reimbursement and holding Dragon Systems harmless, whereas the changed text introduces discretion ('may consider reimbursing') and vagueness ('some liabilities or obligations vaguely related'). This weakens the obligation of MMI. The law explanation states that M.G.L. c. 231A, § 1 allows parties to seek a declaratory judgment to determine their rights, but the ambiguous nature of the altered clause makes it hard to for Dragon Systems to get a clear judgment. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses payment obligations, but does not have significant relevance to the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties shall take reasonable precautions to preserve in strict confidence any confidential or proprietary information obtained by them, their agents or employees concerning the business, products, equipment or services of the other party, including without limitation, trade secrets. Such reasonable precautions shall include exercising precautionary measures designed to preserve the secrecy of such information and to prevent its disclosure to third parties, except following prior consent of the other party, with such precautions being at least equivalent to those taken by each party with respect to its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties should try to take precautions they think are reasonable to generally keep confidential any information they think is confidential, perhaps including trade secrets. Such precautions might include something like keeping the information secret, maybe. It’s okay to tell other people if the other party doesn’t really mind.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear obligation to protect confidential information. The modified version replaces 'shall take reasonable precautions' with 'should try to take precautions they think are reasonable,' making the obligation discretionary and subjective. 'Generally keep confidential', 'they think is confidential', 'perhaps including trade secrets', 'something like keeping the information secret, maybe' and 'if the other party doesn’t really mind' further weaken the obligation, introducing vagueness and uncertainty. This undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93: Regulation of Trade and Certain Enterprises - Protection of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93, § 42 protects trade secrets by defining misappropriation and providing remedies for their unauthorized disclosure or use. The modified confidentiality clause, by making the obligation to protect confidential information vague and discretionary, effectively undermines the protection afforded by this law. The clause's use of subjective language like 'they think are reasonable' and qualifiers like 'generally' and 'maybe' creates uncertainty about the parties' obligations, making it difficult to prove misappropriation or enforce the trade secret protections under the statute. The relaxed standard for disclosure ('if the other party doesn’t really mind') further weakens the protection, allowing for potential disclosures that would violate the statute's prohibition on unauthorized use or disclosure of trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting M.G.L. c. 93, § 42, which protects trade secrets. The original text mandates 'reasonable precautions,' while the changed text uses discretionary language like 'should try' and 'they think are reasonable.' This undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality clause. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' to include unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. By making the obligation vague and subjective, the modified clause makes it difficult to prove misappropriation because the standard of care is no longer clear or objective. The relaxed disclosure standard ('if the other party doesn’t really mind') allows potential disclosures that violate the statute's prohibition on unauthorized use or disclosure.
